one_line_takeaway: Combining physical adversarial patches with natural image noise compounds classifier and object-detector degradation far beyond patch-only attacks, and a ViT-feature + SGD-classifier pipeline (trained on traditional, non-fine-tuned classifiers) generalizes best to unseen patches and noise, per the authors' new patch+noise benchmark dataset and 10-fold cross-validation study.
key_finding: (1) Combining adversarial patches with natural noise substantially worsens degradation versus patch-only attacks: e.g., ResNet-18 robust accuracy falls from 76.08% (clean) to 29.78% (patch-only) to 3.40%/2.86%/1.64% under patch+Gaussian/Shot/Impulse; ResNet-18 attack success rate rises from 46.11% (patch-only) to 77-78% with added noise; SqueezeNet-1.0 falls below 0.5% robust accuracy under any patch+noise combination. ViT-B/16 and Swin-Tiny are markedly more resilient (e.g., ViT-B/16 robust accuracy stays at 62.84-68.23% under patch+noise vs. 82.68% patch-only). (2) In detection, seen-patch settings are nearly solved: VGG16+AdaBoost reaches 99.30% (COCO) / 99.00% (ImageNet) average accuracy, with Random Forest close behind (gap ≤0.30%). (3) In the harder unseen-patch (zero-shot) setting, performance drops sharply and the ranking flips: ViT+SGD is the most generalizable/robust combination, reaching 79.90% (COCO) and 76.90% (ImageNet) average detection accuracy, outperforming VGG+AB style classifiers that excelled in the seen setting; patch1 and patch4 are the easiest-to-generalize-from patches (≥85%/82% average accuracy on COCO/ImageNet respectively). (4) Under noise perturbation, seen-patch detectors degrade only marginally (SGD: 97.8%→95.9% on COCO, 97.9%→94.9% on ImageNet) while unseen-patch detectors drop a further 5-6 points. (5) The proposed ViT+SGD detector outperforms the prior SOTA (Ojaswee et al. 2023's fine-tuned MobileNet-V2/NASNet-Mobile) on unseen-patch and unseen-patch+noise settings except for M-NetV2 on ImageNet, but with markedly lower standard deviation (3.46% vs. the best baseline). (6) The compound patch+noise attack transfers to object detection: Faster R-CNN mAP falls from 36.88 (clean) to 33.62 (patch) to 20.89 (patch+noise); YOLO-v8 falls from 36.69 to 33.41 to 15.87 mAP; YOLO-v11 from 45.66 to 41.51 to 25.54 mAP -- despite patches never being optimized against these detectors/task. (7) Neither standard data augmentation (RandAugment 0.86/0.78, Cutout 0.83/0.81, strong color+geometry 0.86/0.81 on unseen-patch/unseen-patch+noise) nor random-noise-injection training (which actually reduced accuracy from 85.26% to 79.81%) matches or explains the proposed method's robustness (0.85/0.80), leading the authors to attribute gains to their ViT+SGD combination itself rather than augmentation.
core_contribution: The paper's own contributions (stated explicitly in the introduction) are: (i) a first-ever benchmark dataset combining physical adversarial patches with natural noise corruptions (Gaussian, Shot, Impulse), built on ImageNet and COCO images, containing 4,000 clean images, 40,000 adversarial-patch images (10 patch styles), 4,800 noise-only test images, and 48,000 combined patch+noise test images; and (ii) a benchmark study of adversarial-patch detection using deep-network features (VGG16, ViT-B/16) paired with traditional ML classifiers (AdaBoost, SGD, Random Forest, Logistic Regression, SVC) rather than end-to-end neural-network fine-tuning, evaluated across seen-patch, unseen-patch (zero-shot, 10-fold cross-validation), and noise-perturbed settings. The paper also empirically demonstrates (Section 3.1/Section 4.1) that combining adversarial patches with natural noise degrades classifier robust accuracy and object-detector mAP far more than patch-only attacks, and that this transfers to unseen object-detection architectures never used to optimize the patches.
threat_model: Physical-world evasion attacks against deep vision models: (1) adversarial patches (small printed/attached perturbations, from Pintor et al. 2023's ImageNet-Patch, applied to clean images) that are affine-transformation agnostic and were originally optimized against AlexNet/ResNet-18/SqueezeNet but transfer across architectures; (2) natural/environmental noise (Gaussian, Shot, Impulse) that co-occurs with patches in the physical world; and (3) the compound patch+noise attack as an out-of-distribution (OOD) singularity. The defender's goal is binary detection of patch-perturbed vs. clean images, evaluated both when attack patch styles are seen at training time and when they are held out (zero-shot/unseen), with and without added noise. The paper also tests transfer of this same threat to object detection (Faster R-CNN, YOLO-v8/v9/v10/v11) on COCO, an unseen task/architecture relative to the classification-based detectors.
limitations: The paper's own text flags: (a) the released dataset uses only 10 patch styles (from Pintor et al. 2023's ImageNet-Patch) and 3 noise types (Gaussian, Shot, Impulse) at a single fixed severity level (severity=2), which the authors acknowledge is a partial coverage of real-world singularities and plan to expand in future work with 'additional manipulations, including adversarial perturbations'; (b) the detection method is binary (patch vs. clean) classification rather than localization or a certified defense; (c) high seen-patch accuracy is explicitly called out by the authors themselves as potentially giving 'a false sense of security' since future/unseen patch designs cannot be anticipated; (d) the announced future work item (a 'novel attention-guided self-supervised patch-detection algorithm... as a unified defense solution') is stated as not yet delivered in this paper; (e) traditional classifiers were evaluated with scikit-learn default hyperparameters (no tuning reported), and several other classifiers (KNN, decision trees, gradient boosting) were tried but excluded from reported results for being less effective, without quantitative detail; (f) object-detection transfer experiments (Section 4.1) use patches never optimized for detection, which the authors present as a strength (transferability) but also means it does not evaluate patches specifically crafted to attack detectors.

datasets_used: ImageNet (validation subset, 2000 clean images sampled non-cherry-picked)
ImageNet-Patch (10 adversarial patch styles, from Pintor et al. 2023)
Proposed patch+noise singularity dataset: 4,000 clean images, 40,000 adversarial-patch images (20,000 ImageNet + 20,000 COCO), 4,800 noise-only test images, 48,000 combined patch+noise test images
Gaussian, Shot, and Impulse noise corruptions (applied only to test sets)
quantitative_results: Robust accuracy under patch-only vs patch+noise (Table 3): ResNet-18 76.08% clean -> 29.78% patch-only -> 3.40%/2.86%/1.64% under patch+Gaussian/Shot/Impulse; ResNet-50 82.70% clean -> 60.85% patch-only -> 12.49% patch+impulse; ViT-B/16 85.10% clean -> 82.68% patch-only -> 62.84% patch+impulse (most resilient); SqueezeNet-1.0 falls below 0.5% under any patch+noise combo

Attack success rate (Table 4): ResNet-18 46.11% (patch-only) -> 77.20-78.31% (patch+noise); SqueezeNet-1.0 58.00% -> >75%; ViT-B/16 0.71% -> 5.70% (patch+impulse), Swin-Tiny 0.67% -> 10.75% (patch+shot)
Seen-patch detection: VGG16+AdaBoost 99.30% (COCO) and 99.00% (ImageNet) average accuracy; Random Forest within 0.30% of AdaBoost
Unseen-patch detection: ViT+SGD 79.90% (COCO) and 76.90% (ImageNet) average detection accuracy (Fig. 3); patch1/patch4 generalize best (>=85%/82% average accuracy on COCO/ImageNet)
Noise resiliency: seen-patch SGD drops 97.8%->95.9% (COCO) and 97.9%->94.9% (ImageNet) under noise; unseen-patch settings drop a further 5-6 points

Object-detection transfer (Table 9, mAP on COCO): Faster R-CNN(ResNet-50) 36.88 clean -> 33.62 patch -> 20.89 patch+noise; YOLO-v8 36.69 -> 33.41 -> 15.87; YOLO-v9 51.87 -> 47.49 -> 34.70; YOLO-v10 38.22 -> 35.20 -> 19.35; YOLO-v11 45.66 -> 41.51 -> 25.54
Augmentation ablation (Table 10, unseen-patch / unseen-patch+noise accuracy): RandAugment 0.86/0.78, Cutout 0.83/0.81, Strong Color+Geometry 0.86/0.81, Ours (ViT+SGD) 0.85/0.80
Random noise injection during training: accuracy fell from 85.26% to 79.81% on unseen patches/noise, i.e., it hurt rather than helped robustness
SOTA comparison (Fig. 5): proposed ViT+SGD outperforms Ojaswee et al. 2023's fine-tuned MobileNet-V2/NASNet-Mobile except M-NetV2 on ImageNet, with lower STD (3.46% vs. best baseline)
baselines_compared: 10 ImageNet classifiers for attack-effectiveness study: AlexNet, ResNet-18, SqueezeNet-1.0, ResNet-50, MobileNet-V2, VGG-16, ViT-B/16, GoogLeNet, Swin-Tiny, XceptionNet
5 traditional ML classifiers on top of VGG16/ViT features: AdaBoost, SGD, Random Forest, Logistic Regression, SVC (KNN, decision trees, gradient boosting tried but excluded as less effective)
Prior SOTA patch detector: Ojaswee et al. 2023 (fine-tuned MobileNet-V2 and NASNet-Mobile)
Data augmentation baselines: RandAugment, Cutout, strong color+geometry transformations
Random noise-injection training as a robustness baseline
Object detectors for transfer study: Faster R-CNN (ResNet-50 backbone), YOLO-v8, YOLO-v9, YOLO-v10, YOLO-v11
Additional encoders tried but found inferior to VGG: NASNetMobile (VGG outperforms by >=5.4% on COCO/ImageNet), Xception
